question: Distance travelled can be found from the

explanations.A: Correct. The area under a v-t graph gives displacement/distance. Area = velocity × time = distance.
explanations.B: Wrong. The area under an a-t graph gives change in velocity, not distance.
explanations.C: Wrong. The gradient of a force-time graph has no standard kinematic meaning for distance.
explanations.D: Wrong. The gradient of a v-t graph gives acceleration, not distance.

hint: Think about what quantity you get when you multiply velocity by time.

question: Which of the following is a scalar quantity?

explanations.A: Wrong. Acceleration has both magnitude and direction — it's a vector.
explanations.B: Wrong. Displacement specifies a direction — it's a vector.
explanations.C: Wrong. Force acts in a specific direction — it's a vector.
explanations.D: Correct. Work (W = Fs cosθ) is a scalar — it has magnitude only, measured in joules.

hint: Scalars have magnitude only. Which of these doesn't need a direction to be fully described?

question: A car pulls a trailer of weight 2500 N with a force of 20 N for a distance of 8 km along a horizontal road. How much work is done by the car in pulling the trailer?

explanations.A: Wrong. This is 20 × 8 = 160 — you forgot to convert km to m.
explanations.B: Wrong. This is 2500 × 8 — you used the weight instead of the pulling force, and didn't convert km.
explanations.C: Correct. W = Fd = 20 N × 8000 m = 160 000 J. Remember to convert 8 km to 8000 m.
explanations.D: Wrong. This is 2500 × 8000 — you used the weight instead of the pulling force.

hint: Work done = force × distance. Make sure you use the correct force and convert units properly.

question: Which of the following units could be used for power?

explanations.A: Wrong. kg m s⁻² is the unit of force (newton).
explanations.B: Wrong. kg m² s⁻² is the unit of energy (joule).
explanations.C: Correct. Power = energy/time. J/s = kg m² s⁻² / s = kg m² s⁻³ = watt.
explanations.D: Wrong. This has kg², which doesn't correspond to any standard physical quantity for power.

hint: Power = energy ÷ time. Work out the base units of energy first, then divide by seconds.

question: A building has 5 floors. The windows on successive floors are separated by the same vertical distance. A brick is dropped from a window on each floor at the same time. The bricks should hit the ground at

explanations.A: Correct. Using s = ½gt², time ∝ √s. The higher bricks fall further but the time intervals between impacts decrease because the lower bricks have less distance to fall and arrive in quicker succession.
explanations.B: Wrong. Equal intervals would require equal distances, but t ∝ √s means the relationship is non-linear.
explanations.C: Wrong. The intervals actually decrease, not increase.
explanations.D: Wrong. They're dropped from different heights so they can't all hit at the same time.

hint: Use s = ½gt². How does fall time relate to height? Think about the square root relationship.

question: All ductile materials are also

explanations.A: Wrong. Brittle is the opposite of ductile — brittle materials fracture without plastic deformation.
explanations.B: Wrong. Hardness (resistance to scratching) is independent of ductility.
explanations.C: Correct. Ductile means it can be drawn into wires; malleable means it can be hammered into shape. Both require significant plastic deformation, so all ductile materials are also malleable.
explanations.D: Wrong. Stiffness (resistance to elastic deformation) is independent of ductility.

hint: Ductile means a material can undergo significant plastic deformation. What other property requires plastic deformation?

question: An aeroplane is climbing at a constant velocity. Which of the following shows the correct two relationships between the forces?

explanations.A: Correct. At constant velocity climbing: the resultant force is zero. Resolving along and perpendicular to the direction of travel, for a climb the vertical component of thrust plus the vertical component of lift must equal weight, and the horizontal components must balance. Since the plane climbs, lift > weight in the vertical direction, and thrust > drag to maintain the climb angle.
explanations.B: Wrong. If thrust = drag, there would be no component to sustain the climb against gravity's component along the flight path.
explanations.C: Wrong. If lift = weight exactly, the plane couldn't maintain an upward trajectory.
explanations.D: Wrong. This describes level flight at constant speed, not climbing.

hint: Constant velocity means zero resultant force. But the plane is climbing — think about what components of the forces are needed for upward motion.

question: The aeroplane is now flown at a constant altitude but an increasing speed. Which of the following pairs of forces will have the same magnitude?

explanations.A: Wrong. Drag acts horizontally and weight acts vertically — they balance different components.
explanations.B: Wrong. If drag = thrust, there would be no net horizontal force, so no acceleration — but the plane is speeding up.
explanations.C: Wrong. Lift acts vertically and drag acts horizontally — they act in perpendicular directions.
explanations.D: Correct. Constant altitude means no vertical acceleration, so the vertical forces must balance: lift = weight. The plane is accelerating horizontally, so thrust > drag.

hint: Constant altitude means no vertical acceleration. Increasing speed means horizontal acceleration. Which forces are vertical?

question: Which of the following is not a vector quantity?

explanations.A: Wrong. Displacement has direction — it's a vector.
explanations.B: Wrong. Force has direction — it's a vector.
explanations.C: Wrong. Weight acts downward — it's a vector (it's a force).
explanations.D: Correct. Work is a scalar quantity — it has magnitude only (measured in joules).

hint: Vectors have both magnitude and direction. Which quantity here doesn't have a direction?

question: Which of the following units is equivalent to the SI unit for energy?

explanations.A: Wrong. kg m s⁻² is the unit of force (newton), not energy.
explanations.B: Wrong. kW h is a unit of energy but it's not an SI unit — it's a practical unit. The SI unit is the joule.
explanations.C: Wrong. N m⁻¹ is the unit of spring constant (stiffness), not energy.
explanations.D: Correct. Power × time = energy. W × s = J. One watt-second equals one joule.

hint: Energy = power × time. What unit do you get when you multiply watts by seconds?

question: A graph shows how tensile stress varies with tensile strain for a wire, with points X, Y, and Z marked. Which row gives the correct terms for points X, Y, and Z?

options.label: A
options.text: X = Elastic limit, Y = Yield point, Z = Maximum tensile stress

options.label: B
options.text: X = Limit of proportionality, Y = Elastic limit, Z = Yield point

options.label: C
options.text: X = Elastic limit, Y = Maximum tensile stress, Z = Limit of proportionality

options.label: D
options.text: X = Limit of proportionality, Y = Yield point, Z = Maximum tensile stress

explanations.A: Wrong. The elastic limit comes after the limit of proportionality, and Y should be the yield point.
explanations.B: Wrong. Z is at the peak of the curve, which is maximum tensile stress, not the yield point.
explanations.C: Wrong. The order on the graph is: limit of proportionality first, then yield point, then UTS.
explanations.D: Correct. On a stress-strain curve: X (where the line stops being straight) = limit of proportionality, Y (where plastic deformation begins) = yield point, Z (highest point) = maximum tensile stress (UTS).

hint: On a stress-strain graph, the key points in order are: limit of proportionality → yield point → UTS (maximum stress).

question: The acceleration of free fall on Mars is 3.7 m s⁻². If an object on Mars is launched vertically upwards with an initial speed of 40 m s⁻¹, its speed after 3.0 s will be

explanations.A: Wrong. This is 40 − (9.81 × 3) — you used g for Earth, not Mars.
explanations.B: Correct. v = u − at = 40 − (3.7 × 3.0) = 40 − 11.1 = 28.9 ≈ 29 m s⁻¹. The object decelerates due to gravity on Mars.
explanations.C: Wrong. Check your arithmetic — this doesn't match v = u − at with these values.
explanations.D: Wrong. This is 40 + (3.7 × 3) — you added instead of subtracting. The object is decelerating as it goes up.

hint: Use v = u + at. The object is going up and gravity pulls it down, so acceleration is negative.

question: The gravitational field strength on Mars is 3.7 N kg⁻¹. A 5.0 kg object is raised through a height of 150 cm on Mars. The change in gravitational potential energy is

explanations.A: Wrong. This is 3.7 × 5.0 × 1.0 — you may have used 1 m instead of 1.5 m.
explanations.B: Correct. Ep = mgh = 5.0 × 3.7 × 1.50 = 27.75 ≈ 28 J. Remember to convert 150 cm to 1.50 m.
explanations.C: Wrong. This is 5.0 × 9.81 × 1.0 — you used Earth's g and wrong height.
explanations.D: Wrong. This is 5.0 × 9.81 × 1.5 — you used Earth's g instead of Mars's.

hint: Use Ep = mgh. Be careful with units — convert cm to m first.

question: A material which resists plastic deformation by scratching is described as

explanations.A: Wrong. Brittle means a material fractures with little plastic deformation — not about scratching.
explanations.B: Correct. Hardness is defined as resistance to plastic deformation of the surface, typically tested by scratching or indentation.
explanations.C: Wrong. Malleable means a material can be hammered into shape — the opposite of resisting deformation.
explanations.D: Wrong. Stiff means high Young's modulus — resistance to elastic deformation, not scratching.

hint: Which material property is specifically about resistance to surface deformation like scratching?

question: The gradient of a displacement-time graph gives

explanations.A: Wrong. Acceleration is the gradient of a velocity-time graph, not displacement-time.
explanations.B: Wrong. Displacement is read directly from the y-axis, not from the gradient.
explanations.C: Wrong. Force is not directly obtained from a displacement-time graph.
explanations.D: Correct. Velocity = displacement/time = Δs/Δt, which is the gradient of an s-t graph.

hint: Gradient = change in y / change in x. On a displacement-time graph, that's Δs/Δt. What is that?

question: A table tennis ball is released beneath the surface of water and moves upwards. The relationship between the forces acting on the ball when it reaches terminal velocity is

explanations.A: Wrong. If weight = upthrust only, there would be no drag term, which is wrong since the ball is moving.
explanations.B: Correct. The ball moves upward, so upthrust acts upward while weight and drag both act downward. At terminal velocity: upward force = downward forces, so upthrust = weight + drag.
explanations.C: Wrong. This would mean the net force is downward, but the ball is moving upward.
explanations.D: Wrong. This ignores upthrust, which is the driving force pushing the ball upward.

hint: The ball moves UP through water. Upthrust acts UP. Weight and drag both act DOWN. At terminal velocity, forces balance.

question: Which of these quantities is not measured in an SI base unit?

explanations.A: Wrong. Distance is measured in metres — an SI base unit.
explanations.B: Correct. Force is measured in newtons (N), which is a derived unit: N = kg m s⁻². It is NOT a base unit.
explanations.C: Wrong. Mass is measured in kilograms — an SI base unit.
explanations.D: Wrong. Time is measured in seconds — an SI base unit.

hint: The 7 SI base units include metre, kilogram, second, ampere, kelvin, mole, candela. Which quantity here uses a derived unit?

question: Displacement can be found from the

explanations.A: Wrong. The area under a distance-time graph doesn't give a standard kinematic quantity.
explanations.B: Correct. Area under a v-t graph = velocity × time = displacement.
explanations.C: Wrong. The gradient of a distance-time graph gives speed, not displacement.
explanations.D: Wrong. The gradient of a v-t graph gives acceleration, not displacement.

hint: Area under a graph = y-quantity × x-quantity. For a v-t graph, that's velocity × time.

question: A wire of length x is stretched by a force F. The extension is Δx. A second wire of the same material and cross-sectional area is stretched by the same force. If it has twice the length of the first wire its extension will be

explanations.A: Wrong. A longer wire is easier to stretch, not harder.
explanations.B: Wrong. Doubling the length does change the extension.
explanations.C: Correct. From E = FL/AΔL, rearranging gives ΔL = FL/AE. If L doubles (same F, A, E), extension doubles: 2Δx.
explanations.D: Wrong. Extension is proportional to length, not length squared.

hint: Young's modulus E = stress/strain = (F/A)/(ΔL/L). Rearrange for ΔL and see how it depends on L.

question: Which equation shows a scalar quantity as the product of two vector quantities?

explanations.A: Wrong. Power is scalar and time is scalar — this is scalar × scalar = scalar.
explanations.B: Wrong. Stiffness is scalar and extension is scalar (or vector depending on context) — but force is a vector, not scalar, result.
explanations.C: Wrong. Density and volume are both scalars. Mass is also scalar.
explanations.D: Correct. Work = F · s = Fs cosθ. Force is a vector, displacement is a vector, and their dot product (work) is a scalar.

hint: You need: scalar = vector × vector. This is the dot product. Which equation multiplies two directed quantities to give a non-directed result?

question: A material which can be drawn into a wire is described as being

explanations.A: Wrong. Brittle materials fracture easily — they can't be drawn into wires.
explanations.B: Correct. Ductile literally means 'able to be drawn into a wire'. It indicates significant plastic deformation before fracture.
explanations.C: Wrong. Hard means resistant to surface deformation — not related to wire-drawing.
explanations.D: Wrong. Soft means easily deformed — it doesn't specifically mean it can be drawn into a wire.

hint: The definition of this property is literally 'can be drawn into a wire'.

question: A bowling ball of mass 7.0 kg is travelling at a speed of 4.0 m s⁻¹. The kinetic energy of the ball is

explanations.A: Wrong. This is ½ × 7.0 × 4.0 — you forgot to square the velocity.
explanations.B: Wrong. This is 7.0 × 4.0 — you used mv instead of ½mv².
explanations.C: Correct. Ek = ½mv² = ½ × 7.0 × 4.0² = ½ × 7.0 × 16 = 56 J.
explanations.D: Wrong. This is 7.0 × 4.0² — you forgot the ½.

hint: Use Ek = ½mv². Remember to square the velocity before multiplying.

question: An object of weight W sits on an inclined surface at angle θ to the horizontal. The component of the weight W parallel to the surface is

explanations.A: Wrong. There is a component of weight along the slope — that's what makes objects slide.
explanations.B: Wrong. The component depends on W and θ, not just 1.
explanations.C: Wrong. W cos θ is the component perpendicular to the surface (pressing into the slope).
explanations.D: Correct. The component of weight parallel to the slope = W sin θ. This is the force that would cause the object to slide down.

hint: Draw the weight vector straight down, then resolve it into components parallel and perpendicular to the slope.

question: A motor raises a mass m through a height Δh in time t. The power of the motor is given by

explanations.A: Wrong. This gives units of N·s·m which is not watts.
explanations.B: Wrong. This has time in the denominator alongside height — dimensionally incorrect.
explanations.C: Correct. Power = work done / time = (mgΔh) / t. Work done against gravity = mgh.
explanations.D: Wrong. This has height in the denominator — dimensionally incorrect for power.

hint: Power = work done ÷ time. Work done lifting = mgh.

question: Which pair of quantities does not contain a vector and a scalar?

explanations.A: Wrong. Acceleration (vector) and time (scalar) — this pair does contain one of each.
explanations.B: Correct. Force (vector) and displacement (vector) — both are vectors, so this pair does NOT contain a vector and a scalar.
explanations.C: Wrong. Mass (scalar) and acceleration (vector) — this pair does contain one of each.
explanations.D: Wrong. Velocity (vector) and time (scalar) — this pair does contain one of each.

hint: The question asks which pair does NOT have one vector and one scalar. Look for the pair where both are vectors (or both scalars).

question: A wire of length 80 cm has a force F applied. The new length of the wire is 84 cm. The strain is given by

explanations.A: Wrong. You divided by the new length — strain uses the original length.
explanations.B: Correct. Strain = extension / original length = (84 − 80) / 80 = 4/80.
explanations.C: Wrong. This is original length / new length — not the definition of strain.
explanations.D: Wrong. This is new length / original length — not the definition of strain.

hint: Strain = extension ÷ original length. Extension = new length − original length.

question: Which of the following is a derived SI quantity?

explanations.A: Correct. Force (newton) is a derived quantity: N = kg m s⁻². It's made from base units.
explanations.B: Wrong. Length (metre) is a base SI quantity.
explanations.C: Wrong. The second is a base SI unit, not a derived quantity.
explanations.D: Wrong. The watt is a derived unit, but the question asks for a 'derived SI quantity' — force is the quantity, watt is a unit. Both A and D could work, but force is the standard answer as a derived quantity.

hint: Base SI quantities are: mass, length, time, current, temperature, amount, luminous intensity. Everything else is derived.

question: Which of the following descriptions of a material implies that it undergoes significant plastic deformation?

explanations.A: Wrong. Brittle materials undergo very little plastic deformation before fracturing.
explanations.B: Wrong. Hard means resistance to surface deformation — doesn't imply extensive plastic deformation.
explanations.C: Correct. Malleable means a material can be hammered or pressed into shape, which requires significant plastic deformation.
explanations.D: Wrong. Stiff means high Young's modulus — resistance to elastic deformation, not about plastic deformation.

hint: Which property means the material can be reshaped (e.g. hammered into sheets) without breaking?

question: A trolley rolls down a slope from rest. The trolley moves through a vertical height h while rolling a distance s along the slope. The maximum possible speed is given by

explanations.A: Wrong. You should use vertical height h, not slope distance s. Also, this gives v², not v.
explanations.B: Wrong. 2gh gives v², not v. You need to take the square root.
explanations.C: Wrong. The energy depends on vertical height h, not the distance along the slope s.
explanations.D: Correct. By conservation of energy: mgh = ½mv². Solving for v gives v = √(2gh). The vertical height determines the PE lost, not the slope distance.

hint: Use conservation of energy: potential energy lost = kinetic energy gained. PE depends on vertical height, not slope distance.

question: A rocket of mass m lifts off with an acceleration a due to the engines providing a thrust T. Which row correctly identifies m, T and a as scalar or vector?

options.label: A
options.text: m = vector, T = scalar, a = vector

options.label: B
options.text: m = vector, T = scalar, a = scalar

options.label: C
options.text: m = scalar, T = vector, a = vector

options.label: D
options.text: m = scalar, T = vector, a = scalar

explanations.A: Wrong. Mass is a scalar, not a vector.
explanations.B: Wrong. Mass is scalar (correct) but thrust is a vector (it acts in a direction), and acceleration is also a vector.
explanations.C: Correct. Mass is scalar (magnitude only). Thrust is a vector (force has direction). Acceleration is a vector (has direction).
explanations.D: Wrong. Acceleration has both magnitude and direction — it's a vector, not a scalar.

hint: Mass has no direction. Both force (thrust) and acceleration have direction.

question: An increasing force is applied to a spring and the corresponding extension is measured. The spring constant k of the spring is

options.label: A
options.text: the applied force per unit extension

options.label: B
options.text: the applied force per unit length

options.label: C
options.text: the gradient of the extension (y-axis) against force (x-axis) graph

options.label: D
options.text: the area under the extension (y-axis) against force (x-axis) graph

explanations.A: Correct. From F = kx, rearranging gives k = F/x, which is force per unit extension.
explanations.B: Wrong. Force per unit length would involve the total length, not the extension.
explanations.C: Wrong. The gradient of extension vs force gives 1/k (the reciprocal of the spring constant).
explanations.D: Wrong. The area under the extension-force graph gives the elastic strain energy stored.

hint: Hooke's Law: F = kx. Rearrange for k.

question: Select the answer in which both quantities are vectors.

explanations.A: Wrong. Acceleration is a vector but speed is a scalar.
explanations.B: Correct. Displacement (has direction) and velocity (has direction) are both vectors.
explanations.C: Wrong. Mass and time are both scalars.
explanations.D: Wrong. Power is a scalar; weight is a vector.

hint: Look for the pair where BOTH quantities have a direction associated with them.

question: A tennis ball hits a wall perpendicularly at a speed of 4 m s⁻¹ and rebounds at the same speed. Taking the initial velocity as positive, the change in velocity is

explanations.A: Wrong. This only accounts for losing the initial velocity, not gaining velocity in the opposite direction.
explanations.B: Correct. Change in velocity = final − initial = (−4) − (+4) = −8 m s⁻¹. The ball reverses direction, so the change is twice the speed in the negative direction.
explanations.C: Wrong. The speed is the same but velocity has changed direction — the change is not zero.
explanations.D: Wrong. The magnitude is correct (8) but the sign should be negative since the ball reverses direction.

hint: Δv = v_final − v_initial. If initial velocity is +4, the rebound velocity is −4. What's the difference?

question: Physical quantities are either vectors or scalars. Select the row which correctly identifies mass, velocity, and displacement as vector or scalar.

options.label: A
options.text: mass = scalar, velocity = vector, displacement = scalar

options.label: B
options.text: mass = vector, velocity = scalar, displacement = vector

options.label: C
options.text: mass = vector, velocity = scalar, displacement = scalar

options.label: D
options.text: mass = scalar, velocity = vector, displacement = vector

explanations.A: Wrong. Displacement has a direction — it's a vector, not a scalar.
explanations.B: Wrong. Mass has no direction (scalar), and velocity has direction (vector) — both are wrong here.
explanations.C: Wrong. Mass is scalar (correct), but velocity is a vector (wrong here).
explanations.D: Correct. Mass = scalar (magnitude only), velocity = vector (magnitude + direction), displacement = vector (magnitude + direction).

hint: Mass has no direction. Velocity and displacement both specify a direction.

question: A force is applied to a length of wire. Which of the following statements is not correct for small deformations of the wire?

options.label: A
options.text: As the force applied increases, the extension increases

options.label: B
options.text: The force applied is directly proportional to the extension

options.label: C
options.text: The force applied is directly proportional to the original length

options.label: D
options.text: The stress is directly proportional to the strain

explanations.A: This IS correct for small deformations — Hooke's law applies.
explanations.B: This IS correct — F = kx shows direct proportionality within the elastic limit.
explanations.C: This is NOT correct. The force is not proportional to the original length — the extension depends on original length (longer wire stretches more for same force), but force isn't proportional to length.
explanations.D: This IS correct — within the limit of proportionality, stress ∝ strain (Young's modulus is constant).

hint: Think about each statement carefully. Which one doesn't follow from Hooke's Law or the definition of Young's modulus?

question: Aluminium can be used to produce thin sheets of food wrapping because it is

explanations.A: Wrong. Brittle materials shatter — they can't be rolled into thin sheets.
explanations.B: Wrong. Ductile means it can be drawn into wires, not rolled into sheets.
explanations.C: Wrong. Hardness is about resistance to scratching — not about forming thin sheets.
explanations.D: Correct. Malleable means a material can be hammered or rolled into thin sheets without cracking. This is exactly what's done to make aluminium foil.

hint: Which property describes the ability to be pressed or rolled into thin sheets?

question: A stone dropped into a well takes 1.5 seconds to reach the water. Ignoring the effects of air resistance, what distance did the stone fall through?

explanations.A: Wrong. This is approximately ½ × 9.81 × 1.0² — you may have used the wrong time.
explanations.C: Wrong. This is approximately 9.81 × 1.5 — you used s = gt instead of s = ½gt².
explanations.D: Wrong. This is approximately 9.81 × 1.5² — you forgot the ½.

hint: Dropped from rest: u = 0. Use s = ½gt². Don't forget the ½!

question: Which of the following is equivalent to the joule in terms of SI base units?

explanations.A: Wrong. kg m² s⁻³ is the unit of power (watt), not energy.
explanations.B: Correct. Energy (J) = force × distance = (kg m s⁻²) × m = kg m² s⁻².
explanations.C: Wrong. kg m s⁻² is the unit of force (newton), not energy.
explanations.D: Wrong. kg m s⁻¹ is the unit of momentum, not energy.

hint: J = N × m. Write the newton in base units first, then multiply by metres.

question: A wind turbine generates 550 W of electrical power for an average of 7 hours each day. What is the total energy, in MJ, generated each day?

explanations.A: Wrong. Check your unit conversion — this is too small.
explanations.C: Wrong. You may have forgotten to convert hours to seconds correctly.
explanations.D: Wrong. You may have calculated in kJ or used the wrong conversion factor.

hint: E = Pt. Convert 7 hours to seconds (× 3600), then convert joules to MJ (÷ 10⁶).

question: A ball of mass m falls through a height h to the ground. What is the kinetic energy of the ball halfway to the ground?

explanations.A: Wrong. mgh is the total KE at the bottom, not halfway.
explanations.B: Correct. At half the height, the ball has lost half its PE. KE gained = mgh/2.
explanations.C: Wrong. This has wrong units/dimensions — kinetic energy should be in joules, not √(joules).
explanations.D: Wrong. Same dimensional error — you can't take the square root of energy to get energy.

hint: By conservation of energy, KE gained = PE lost. Halfway down, how much PE has been lost?

question: A car travels at a speed of 20 m s⁻¹ due east and then turns around and travels at a speed of 40 m s⁻¹ due west. Taking the direction of due east as positive, select the correct row for change in speed and change in velocity.

options.label: A
options.text: Change in speed = 20, Change in velocity = −60

options.label: B
options.text: Change in speed = 20, Change in velocity = 60

options.label: C
options.text: Change in speed = 60, Change in velocity = −60

options.label: D
options.text: Change in speed = 60, Change in velocity = 60

explanations.A: Correct. Change in speed = 40 − 20 = 20 m s⁻¹ (speed is scalar, always positive). Change in velocity = (−40) − (+20) = −60 m s⁻¹ (velocity is vector, direction matters).
explanations.B: Wrong. The change in velocity should be negative (from positive to negative direction).
explanations.C: Wrong. Change in speed = |40| − |20| = 20, not 60. Speed doesn't include direction.
explanations.D: Wrong. Both values are incorrect for the reasons above.

hint: Speed is scalar (no direction): change = 40 − 20. Velocity is vector: east = +20, west = −40. Change = final − initial.

question: The correct definition of the term centre of gravity is the point at which

options.label: A
options.text: all of the force acts on a body

options.label: C
options.text: the weight of a body may be considered to act

explanations.A: Wrong. Forces can act at many points — this is too vague.
explanations.B: Wrong. Gravity acts throughout the entire body, not at a single point.
explanations.C: Correct. The centre of gravity is the point where the entire weight of the body may be considered to act for the purpose of calculating moments and equilibrium.
explanations.D: Wrong. Weight isn't literally concentrated at one point — it's distributed throughout the body.

hint: It's the point where we can treat the entire weight as acting — for modelling purposes.

question: In the manufacture of cars, mild steel sheets are formed into panels of an appropriate shape. Mild steel can be shaped in this way because it is

explanations.A: Wrong. Brittle materials crack when you try to reshape them.
explanations.B: Wrong. Hard means resistant to scratching — not about reshaping.
explanations.C: Correct. Malleable means a material can be pressed, rolled, or hammered into different shapes. This is exactly what happens when forming car body panels.
explanations.D: Wrong. Strong means resistant to breaking under large forces — not about reshaping.

hint: Forming metal sheets into shapes requires the material to deform plastically without cracking.
